I found comprehensive information about PyTorch Lightning performance optimization with mixed precision. Here are the key findings:

## Mixed Precision Training

Mixed precision combines the use of both FP32 and lower bit floating points (such as FP16) to reduce memory footprint during model training, resulting in improved performance. Supported torch operations are automatically run in FP16, saving memory and improving throughput on GPU and TPU accelerators.

## How It Works

Numerical instability from FP16 computation is handled internally by a dynamic grad scaler which skips steps that are invalid, and adjusts the scaler to ensure subsequent steps fall within a finite range.

## Implementation Options

Lightning offers mixed precision training for GPUs and CPUs, as well as bfloat16 mixed precision training for TPUs. Under the hood, PyTorch Lightning uses torch.autocast with the dtype set to bfloat16, with no gradient scaling.

## Performance Benefits

Since float16 and bfloat16 data types are only half the size of float32 they can double the performance of bandwidth-bound kernels and reduce the memory required to train a network.

## BFloat16 Advantages

BFloat16 mixed precision is similar to FP16 mixed precision, however, it maintains more of the "dynamic range" that FP32 offers, enabling better numerical stability than FP16 mixed precision.

The search results provided comprehensive documentation on mixed precision training, though specific details about `torch.compile` integration were not prominently featured in the results.

content.text: "{\"title\":\"N-Bit Precision (Intermediate) — PyTorch Lightning 2.6.1 documentation\",\"url\":\"https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable/common/precision_intermediate.html\",\"content\":\"__Audience:__ Users looking to scale larger models or take advantage of optimized accelerators.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## What is Mixed Precision?¶\\n\\nPyTorch, like most deep learning frameworks, trains on 32-bit floating-point (FP32) arithmetic by default. However, many deep learning models do not require this to reach complete accuracy. By conducting\\noperations in half-precision format while keeping minimum information in single-precision to maintain as much information as possible in crucial areas of the network, mixed precision training delivers\\nsignificant computational speedup. Switching to mixed precision has resulted in considerable training speedups since the introduction of Tensor Cores in the Volta and Turing architectures. It combines\\nFP32 and lower-bit floating-points (such as FP16) to reduce memory footprint and increase performance during model training and evaluation. It accomplishes this by recognizing the steps that require\\ncomplete accuracy and employing a 32-bit floating-point for those steps only, while using a 16-bit floating-point for the rest. When compared to complete precision training, mixed precision training\\ndelivers all of these benefits while ensuring that no task-specific accuracy is lost. [2].\\n\\nNote\\n\\nIn some cases, it is essential to remain in FP32 for numerical stability, so keep this in mind when using mixed precision.\\nFor example, when running scatter operations during the forward (such as torchpoint3d), computation must remain in FP32.\\n\\nWarning\\n\\nDo not cast anything to other dtypes manually using `torch.autocast` or `tensor.half()` when using native precision because\\nthis can bring instability.\\n\\n```\\nclass LitModel(LightningModule):\\n    def training_step(self, batch, batch_idx):\\n        outs = self(batch)\\n\\n        a_float32 = torch.rand((8, 8), device=self.device, dtype=self.dtype)\\n        b_float32 = torch.rand((8, 4), device=self.device, dtype=self.dtype)\\n\\n        # casting to float16 manually\\n        with torch.autocast(device_type=self.device.type):\\n            c_float16 = torch.mm(a_float32, b_float32)\\n            target = self.layer(c_float16.flatten()[None])\\n\\n        # here outs is of type float32 and target is of type float16\\n        loss = torch.mm(target @ outs).float()\\n        return loss\\n\\ntrainer = Trainer(accelerator=\\\"gpu\\\", devices=1, precision=32)\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## BFloat16 Mixed Precision¶\\n\\nWarning\\n\\nBFloat16 may not provide significant speedups or memory improvements or offer better numerical stability.\\nFor GPUs, the most significant benefits require Ampere based GPUs or newer, such as A100s or 3090s.\\n\\nBFloat16 Mixed precision is similar to FP16 mixed precision, however, it maintains more of the “dynamic range” that FP32 offers. This means it is able to improve numerical stability than FP16 mixed precision. For more information, see this TPU performance blogpost.\\n\\nUnder the hood, we use torch.autocast with the dtype set to `bfloat16`, with no gradient scaling.\\n\\n```\\nTrainer(accelerator=\\\"gpu\\\", devices=1, precision=\\\"bf16-mixed\\\")\\n```\\n\\nIt is also possible to use BFloat16 mixed precision on the CPU, relying on MKLDNN under the hood.\\n\\n```\\nTrainer(precision=\\\"bf16-mixed\\\")\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## True Half Precision¶\\n\\nAs mentioned before, for numerical stability mixed precision keeps the model weights in full float32 precision while casting only supported operations to lower bit precision.\\nHowever, in some cases it is indeed possible to train completely in half precision. Similarly, for inference the model weights can often be cast to half precision without a loss in accuracy (even when trained with mixed precision).\\n\\n```\\n# Select FP16 precision\\ntrainer = Trainer(precision=\\\"16-true\\\")\\ntrainer.fit(model)  # model gets cast to torch.float16\\n\\n# Select BF16 precision\\ntrainer = Trainer(precision=\\\"bf16-true\\\")\\ntrainer.fit(model)  # model gets cast to torch.bfloat16\\n```\\n\\nTip: For faster initialization, you can create model parameters with the desired dtype directly on the device:\\n\\n```\\ntrainer = Trainer(precision=\\\"bf16-true\\\")\\n\\n# init the model directly on the device and with parameters in half-precision\\nwith trainer.init_module():\\n    model = MyModel()\\n\\ntrainer.fit(model)\\n```\\n\\nSee also: Efficient initialization\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Float8 Mixed Precision via Nvidia’s TransformerEngine¶\\n\\nTransformer Engine (TE) is a library for accelerating models on the\\nlatest NVIDIA GPUs using 8-bit floating point (FP8) precision on Hopper GPUs, to provide better performance with lower\\nmemory utilization in both training and inference. It offers improved performance over half precision with no degradation in accuracy.\\n\\nUsing TE requires replacing some of the layers in your model. Fabric automatically replaces the `torch.nn.Linear`\\nand `torch.nn.LayerNorm` layers in your model with their TE alternatives, however, TE also offers\\nfused layers\\nto squeeze out all the possible performance. If Fabric detects that any layer has been replaced already, automatic\\nreplacement is not done.\\n\\nThis plugin is a combination of “mixed” and “true” precision. The computation is downcasted to FP8 precision on the fly, but\\nthe model and inputs can be kept in true full or half precision.\\n\\n```\\n# Select 8bit mixed precision via TransformerEngine, with model weights in bfloat16\\ntrainer = Trainer(precision=\\\"transformer-engine\\\")\\n\\n# Select 8bit mixed precision via TransformerEngine, with model weights in float16\\ntrainer = Trainer(precision=\\\"transformer-engine-float16\\\")\\n\\n# Customize the fp8 recipe or set a different base precision:\\nfrom lightning.trainer.plugins import TransformerEnginePrecision\\n\\nrecipe = {\\\"fp8_format\\\": \\\"HYBRID\\\", \\\"amax_history_len\\\": 16, \\\"amax_compute_algo\\\": \\\"max\\\"}\\nprecision = TransformerEnginePrecision(weights_dtype=torch.bfloat16, recipe=recipe)\\ntrainer = Trainer(plugins=precision)\\n```\\n\\nUnder the hood, we use transformer_engine.pytorch.fp8_autocast with the default fp8 recipe.\\n\\nNote\\n\\nThis requires Hopper based GPUs or newer, such the H100.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Quantization via Bitsandbytes¶\\n\\nbitsandbytes (BNB) is a library that supports quantizing `torch.nn.Linear` weights.\\n\\nBoth 4-bit (paper reference) and 8-bit (paper reference) quantization is supported.\\nSpecifically, we support the following modes:\\n\\n- __nf4__: Uses the normalized float 4-bit data type. This is recommended over “fp4” based on the paper’s experimental results and theoretical analysis.\\n- __nf4-dq__: “dq” stands for “Double Quantization” which reduces the average memory footprint by quantizing the quantization constants. In average, this amounts to about 0.37 bits per parameter (approximately 3 GB for a 65B model).\\n- __fp4__: Uses regular float 4-bit data type.\\n- __fp4-dq__: “dq” stands for “Double Quantization” which reduces the average memory footprint by quantizing the quantization constants. In average, this amounts to about 0.37 bits per parameter (approximately 3 GB for a 65B model).\\n- __int8__: Uses unsigned int8 data type.\\n- __int8-training__: Meant for int8 activations with fp16 precision weights.\\n\\nWhile these techniques store weights in 4 or 8 bit, the computation still happens in 16 or 32-bit (float16, bfloat16, float32).\\nThis is configurable via the dtype argument in the plugin.\\nIf your model weights can fit on a single device with 16 bit precision, it’s recommended that this plugin is not used as it will slow down training.\\n\\nQuantizing the model will dramatically reduce the weight’s memory requirements but may have a negative impact on the model’s performance or runtime.\\n\\nThe `BitsandbytesPrecision` automatically replaces the `torch.nn.Linear` layers in your model with their BNB alternatives.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.plugins import BitsandbytesPrecision\\n\\n# this will pick out the compute dtype automatically, by default `bfloat16`\\nprecision = BitsandbytesPrecision(mode=\\\"nf4-dq\\\")\\ntrainer = Trainer(plugins=precision)\\n\\n# Customize the dtype, or skip some modules\\nprecision = BitsandbytesPrecision(mode=\\\"int8-training\\\", dtype=torch.float16, ignore_modules={\\\"lm_head\\\"})\\ntrainer = Trainer(plugins=precision)\\n\\nclass MyModel(LightningModule):\\n    def configure_model(self):\\n        # instantiate your model in this hook\\n        self.model = MyModel()\\n```\\n\\nNote\\n\\nOnly supports CUDA devices and the Linux operating system. Windows users should use\\nWSL2.\\n\\nThis plugin does not take care of replacing your optimizer with an 8-bit optimizer e.g. `bitsandbytes.optim.Adam8bit`.\\nYou might want to do this for extra memory savings.\\n\\n```\\nimport bitsandbytes as bnb\\n\\nclass MyModel(LightningModule):\\n    def configure_optimizers(self):\\n        optimizer = bnb.optim.Adam8bit(model.parameters(), lr=0.001, betas=(0.9, 0.995))\\n\\n        # (optional) force embedding layers to use 32 bit for numerical stability\\n        # https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/issues/14819#issuecomment-1003445038\\n        for module in model.modules():\\n            if isinstance(module, torch.nn.Embedding):\\n                bnb.optim.GlobalOptimManager.get_instance().register_module_override(\\n                    module, \\\"weight\\\", {\\\"optim_bits\\\": 32}\\n                )\\n\\n        return optimizer\\n```\",\"metadata\":{\"google-site-verification\":\"okUst94cAlWSsUsGZTB4xSS4UKTtRV8Nu5XZ9pdd3Aw\",\"viewport\":\"width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0\",\"generator\":\"Docutils 0.19: https://docutils.sourceforge.io/\",\"lang\":\"en\"},\"external\":{\"stylesheet\":{\"https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto:wght@100;300;400;500;700&display=swap\":{},\"../_static/main.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/css/theme.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/sphinx_paramlinks.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/copybutton.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/togglebutton.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/pygments.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"}},\"preconnect\":{\"https://fonts.gstatic.com\":{},\"https://fonts.googleapis.com\":{}},\"search\":{\"../search.html\":{\"title\":\"Search\"}},\"shortcut\":{\"../_static/icon.svg\":{}},\"index\":{\"../genindex.html\":{\"title\":\"Index\"}},\"canonical\":{\"https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable//common/precision_intermediate.html\":{}},\"preload\":{\"https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.10.0/dist/fonts/KaTeX_Size4-Regular.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.10.0/dist/fonts/KaTeX_Caligraphic-Regular.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/FreightSans/freight-sans-bold.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/FreightSans/freight-sans-book.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.10.0/dist/fonts/KaTeX_Main-Bold.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/IBMPlexMono/IBMPlexMono-SemiBold.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/FreightSans/freight-sans-medium.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/UCity/UCity-Light.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.10.0/dist/fonts/KaTeX_Main-Regular.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.10.0/dist/fonts/KaTeX_Size1-Regular.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/UCity/UCity-Regular.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/Inconsolata/Inconsolata.woff2\":{\"type\":\"font/woff2\"},\"../_static/fonts/FreightSans/freight-sans-m "{\"title\":\"Speed up model training — PyTorch Lightning 1.5.10 documentation\",\"url\":\"https://pytorch-lightning.readthedocs.io/en/1.5.10/guides/speed.html\",\"content\":\"There are multiple ways you can speed up your model’s time to convergence:\\n\\n- gpu/tpu training\\n- mixed precision (16-bit) training\\n- control training epochs\\n- control validation frequency\\n- limit dataset size\\n- preload data into ram\\n- model toggling\\n- set grads to none\\n- things to avoid\\n\\n## GPU/TPU training¶\\n\\n__Use when:__ Whenever possible!\\n\\nWith Lightning, running on GPUs, TPUs or multiple node is a simple switch of a flag.\\n\\n### GPU training¶\\n\\nLightning supports a variety of plugins to further speed up distributed GPU training. Most notably:\\n\\n- `DDPPlugin`\\n- `DDPShardedPlugin`\\n- `DeepSpeedPlugin`\\n\\n```\\n# run on 1 gpu\\ntrainer = Trainer(gpus=1)\\n\\n# train on 8 gpus, using the DDP strategy\\ntrainer = Trainer(gpus=8, strategy=\\\"ddp\\\")\\n\\n# train on multiple GPUs across nodes (uses 8 gpus in total)\\ntrainer = Trainer(gpus=2, num_nodes=4)\\n```\\n\\n#### GPU Training Speedup Tips¶\\n\\nWhen training on single or multiple GPU machines, Lightning offers a host of advanced optimizations to improve throughput, memory efficiency, and model scaling.\\nRefer to Advanced GPU Optimized Training for more details.\\n\\n##### Prefer DDP over DP¶\\n\\n`DataParallelPlugin` performs three GPU transfers for EVERY batch:\\n\\n1. Copy model to device.\\n2. Copy data to device.\\n3. Copy outputs of each device back to master.\\n\\nWhereas `DDPPlugin` only performs 1 transfer to sync gradients, making DDP MUCH faster than DP.\\n\\n##### When using DDP plugins, set find_unused_parameters=False¶\\n\\nBy default we have set `find_unused_parameters` to True for compatibility reasons that have been observed in the past (see the discussion for more details).\\nThis by default comes with a performance hit, and can be disabled in most cases.\\n\\nTip\\n\\nIt applies to all DDP plugins that support `find_unused_parameters` as input.\\n\\n```\\nfrom pytorch_lightning.plugins import DDPPlugin\\n\\ntrainer = pl.Trainer(\\n    gpus=2,\\n    strategy=DDPPlugin(find_unused_parameters=False),\\n)\\n```\\n\\n```\\nfrom pytorch_lightning.plugins import DDPSpawnPlugin\\n\\ntrainer = pl.Trainer(\\n    gpus=2,\\n    strategy=DDPSpawnPlugin(find_unused_parameters=False),\\n)\\n```\\n\\n##### When using DDP on a multi-node cluster, set NCCL parameters¶\\n\\nNCCL is the NVIDIA Collective Communications Library which is used under the hood by PyTorch to handle communication across nodes and GPUs. There are reported benefits in terms of speedups when adjusting NCCL parameters as seen in this issue. In the issue we see a 30% speed improvement when training the Transformer XLM-RoBERTa and a 15% improvement in training with Detectron2.\\n\\nNCCL parameters can be adjusted via environment variables.\\n\\nNote\\n\\nAWS and GCP already set default values for these on their clusters. This is typically useful for custom cluster setups.\\n\\n- NCCL_NSOCKS_PERTHREAD\\n- NCCL_SOCKET_NTHREADS\\n- NCCL_MIN_NCHANNELS\\n\\n```\\nexport NCCL_NSOCKS_PERTHREAD=4\\nexport NCCL_SOCKET_NTHREADS=2\\n```\\n\\n##### Dataloaders¶\\n\\nWhen building your DataLoader set `num_workers > 0` and `pin_memory=True` (only for GPUs).\\n\\n```\\nDataloader(dataset, num_workers=8, pin_memory=True)\\n```\\n\\n###### num_workers¶\\n\\nThe question of how many workers to specify in `num_workers` is tricky. Here’s a summary of\\nsome references, [1], and our suggestions:\\n\\n1. `num_workers=0` means ONLY the main process will load batches (that can be a bottleneck).\\n2. `num_workers=1` means ONLY one worker (just not the main process) will load data but it will still be slow.\\n3. The `num_workers` depends on the batch size and your machine.\\n4. A general place to start is to set `num_workers` equal to the number of CPU cores on that machine. You can get the number of CPU cores in python using os.cpu_count(), but note that depending on your batch size, you may overflow RAM memory.\\n\\nWarning\\n\\nIncreasing `num_workers` will ALSO increase your CPU memory consumption.\\n\\nThe best thing to do is to increase the `num_workers` slowly and stop once you see no more improvement in your training speed.\\n\\nFor debugging purposes or for dataloaders that load very small datasets, it is desirable to set `num_workers=0`. However, this will always log a warning for every dataloader with `num_workers <= min(2, os.cpu_count())`. In such cases, you can specifically filter this warning by using:\\n\\n```\\nimport warnings\\n\\nwarnings.filterwarnings(\\\"ignore\\\", \\\".*Consider increasing the value of the `num_workers` argument*\\\")\\n```\\n\\n###### Spawn¶\\n\\nWhen using `strategy=ddp_spawn` or training on TPUs, the way multiple GPUs/TPU cores are used is by calling `.spawn()` under the hood.\\nThe problem is that PyTorch has issues with `num_workers > 0` when using `.spawn()`. For this reason we recommend you\\nuse `strategy=ddp` so you can increase the `num_workers`, however your script has to be callable like so:\\n\\n### TPU training¶\\n\\nYou can set the `tpu_cores` trainer flag to 1 or 8 cores.\\n\\n```\\n# train on 1 TPU core\\ntrainer = Trainer(tpu_cores=1)\\n\\n# train on 8 TPU cores\\ntrainer = Trainer(tpu_cores=8)\\n```\\n\\nTo train on more than 8 cores (ie: a POD),\\nsubmit this script using the xla_dist script.\\n\\nExample:\\n\\n```\\npython -m torch_xla.distributed.xla_dist\\n--tpu=$TPU_POD_NAME\\n--conda-env=torch-xla-nightly\\n--env=XLA_USE_BF16=1\\n-- python your_trainer_file.py\\n```\\n\\nRead more in our Accelerators and Plugins guides.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Mixed precision (16-bit) training¶\\n\\n__Use when:__\\n\\n- You want to optimize for memory usage on a GPU.\\n- You have a GPU that supports 16 bit precision (NVIDIA pascal architecture or newer).\\n- Your optimization algorithm (training_step) is numerically stable.\\n- You want to be the cool person in the lab :p\\n\\n[![](https://pl-bolts-doc-images.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/pl_docs/trainer_flags/yt_thumbs/thumb_precision.png)](https://pl-bolts-doc-images.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/pl_docs/yt/Trainer+flags+9+-+precision_1.mp4)\\n\\nMixed precision combines the use of both 32 and 16 bit floating points to reduce memory footprint during model training, resulting in improved performance, achieving +3X speedups on modern GPUs.\\n\\nLightning offers mixed precision training for GPUs and CPUs, as well as bfloat16 mixed precision training for TPUs.\\n\\n```\\n# 16-bit precision\\ntrainer = Trainer(precision=16, gpus=4)\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Control Training Epochs¶\\n\\n__Use when:__ You run a hyperparameter search to find good initial parameters and want to save time, cost (money), or power (environment).\\nIt can allow you to be more cost efficient and also run more experiments at the same time.\\n\\nYou can use Trainer flags to force training for a minimum number of epochs or limit to a max number of epochs. Use the min_epochs and max_epochs Trainer flags to set the number of epochs to run.\\n\\n```\\n# DEFAULT\\ntrainer = Trainer(min_epochs=1, max_epochs=1000)\\n```\\n\\nIf running iteration based training, i.e. infinite / iterable dataloader, you can also control the number of steps with the min_steps and max_steps flags:\\n\\n```\\ntrainer = Trainer(max_steps=1000)\\n\\ntrainer = Trainer(min_steps=100)\\n```\\n\\nYou can also interupt training based on training time:\\n\\n```\\n# Stop after 12 hours of training or when reaching 10 epochs (string)\\ntrainer = Trainer(max_time=\\\"00:12:00:00\\\", max_epochs=10)\\n\\n# Stop after 1 day and 5 hours (dict)\\ntrainer = Trainer(max_time={\\\"days\\\": 1, \\\"hours\\\": 5})\\n```\\n\\nLearn more in our Trainer flags guide.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Control Validation Frequency¶\\n\\n### Check validation every n epochs¶\\n\\n__Use when:__ You have a small dataset, and want to run less validation checks.\\n\\nYou can limit validation check to only run every n epochs using the check_val_every_n_epoch Trainer flag.\\n\\n```\\n# DEFAULT\\ntrainer = Trainer(check_val_every_n_epoch=1)\\n```\\n\\n### Set validation check frequency within 1 training epoch¶\\n\\n__Use when:__ You have a large training dataset, and want to run mid-epoch validation checks.\\n\\nFor large datasets, it’s often desirable to check validation multiple times within a training loop.\\nPass in a float to check that often within 1 training epoch. Pass in an int k to check every k training batches.\\nMust use an int if using an IterableDataset.\\n\\n```\\n# DEFAULT\\ntrainer = Trainer(val_check_interval=0.95)\\n\\n# check every .25 of an epoch\\ntrainer = Trainer(val_check_interval=0.25)\\n\\n# check every 100 train batches (ie: for `IterableDatasets` or fixed frequency)\\ntrainer = Trainer(val_check_interval=100)\\n```\\n\\nLearn more in our Trainer flags guide.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Limit Dataset Size¶\\n\\n### Use data subset for training, validation, and test¶\\n\\n__Use when:__ Debugging or running huge datasets.\\n\\nIf you don’t want to check 100% of the training/validation/test set set these flags:\\n\\n```\\n# DEFAULT\\ntrainer = Trainer(limit_train_batches=1.0, limit_val_batches=1.0, limit_test_batches=1.0)\\n\\n# check 10%, 20%, 30% only, respectively for training, validation and test set\\ntrainer = Trainer(limit_train_batches=0.1, limit_val_batches=0.2, limit_test_batches=0.3)\\n```\\n\\nIf you also pass `shuffle=True` to the dataloader, a different random subset of your dataset will be used for each epoch; otherwise the same subset will be used for all epochs.\\n\\nNote\\n\\n`limit_train_batches`, `limit_val_batches` and `limit_test_batches` will be overwritten by `overfit_batches` if `overfit_batches` > 0. `limit_val_batches` will be ignored if `fast_dev_run=True`.\\n\\nNote\\n\\nIf you set `limit_val_batches=0`, validation will be disabled.\\n\\nLearn more in our Trainer flags guide.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Preload Data Into RAM¶\\n\\n__Use when:__ You need access to all samples in a dataset at once.\\n\\nWhen your training or preprocessing requires many operations to be performed on entire dataset(s), it can\\nsometimes be beneficial to store all data in RAM given there is enough space.\\nHowever, loading all data at the beginning of the training script has the disadvantage that it can take a long\\ntime and hence it slows down the development process. Another downside is that in multiprocessing (e.g. DDP)\\nthe data would get copied in each process.\\nOne can overcome these problems by copying the data into RAM in advance.\\nMost UNIX-based operating systems provide direct access to tmpfs through a mount point typically named `/dev/shm`.\\n\\n0. Increase shared memory if necessary. Refer to the documentation of your OS how to do this.\\n1. Copy training data to shared memory:\\n\\n   ```\\n   cp -r /path/to/data/on/disk /dev/shm/\\n   ```\\n2. Refer to the new data root in your script or command line arguments:\\n\\n   ```\\n   datamodule = MyDataModule(data_root=\\\"/dev/shm/my_data\\\")\\n   ```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Model Toggling¶\\n\\n__Use when:__ Performing gradient accumulation with multiple optimizers in a\\ndistributed setting.\\n\\nHere is an explanation of what it does:\\n\\n- Considering the current optimizer as A and all other optimizers as B.\\n- Toggling means that all parameters from B exclusive to A will have their `requires_grad` attribute set to `False`.\\n- Their original state will be restored when exiting the context manager.\\n\\nWhen performing gradient accumulation, there is no need to perform grad synchronization during the accumulation phase.\\nSetting `sync_grad` to `False` will block this synchronization and improve your training speed.\\n\\n`LightningOptimizer` provides a\\n`toggle_model()` function as a\\n`contextlib.contextmanager()` for advanced users.\\n\\nHere is an example for advanced use-case:\\n\\n```\\n# Scenario for a GAN with gradient accumulation every 2 batches and optimized for multiple gpus.\\nclass SimpleGAN(LightningModule):\\n    def __init__(self):\\n        super().__init__()\\n        self.automatic_optimization = False\\n\\n    def training_step(self, batch, batch_idx):\\n        # Implementation follows the PyTorch tutorial:\\n        # https://pytorch.org/tutorials/beginner/dcgan_faces_tutorial.html\\n        g_opt, d_opt = self.optimizers()\\n\\n        X, _ = batch\\n        X.requires_grad = True\\n        batch_size = X.shape[0]\\n\\n        real_label = torch.ones((batch_size, 1), device=self.device)\\n        fake_label = torch.zeros((batch_size, 1), device=self.device)\\n\\n        # Sync and clear gradients\\n        # at the end of accumulation or\\n        # at the end of an epoch.\\n        is_last_batch_to_accumulate = (batch_idx + 1) % 2 == 0 or self.trainer.is_last_batch\\n\\n        g_X = self.sample_G(batch_size)\\n\\n        ##########################\\n        # Optimize Discriminator #\\n        ##########################\\n        with d_opt.toggle_model(sync_grad=is_last_batch_to_accumulate):\\n            d_x = self.D(X)\\n            errD_real = self.criterion(d_x, real_label)\\n\\n            d_z = self.D(g_X.detach())\\n            errD_fake = self.criterion(d_z, fake_label)\\n\\n            errD = errD_real + errD_fake\\n\\n            self.manual_backward(errD)\\n            if is_last_batch_to_accumulate:\\n                d_opt.step()\\n                d_opt.zero_grad()\\n\\n        ######################\\n        # Optimize Generator #\\n        ######################\\n        with g_opt.toggle_model(sync_grad=is_last_batch_to_accumulate):\\n            d_z = self.D(g_X)\\n            errG = self.criterion(d_z, real_label)\\n\\n            self.manual_backward(errG)\\n            if is_last_batch_to_accumulate:\\n                g_opt.step()\\n                g_opt.zero_grad()\\n\\n        self.log_dict({\\\"g_loss\\\": errG, \\\"d_loss\\\": errD}, prog_bar=True)\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Set Grads to None¶\\n\\nIn order to modestly improve performance, you can override `optimizer_zero_grad()`.\\n\\nFor a more detailed explanation of pros / cons of this technique,\\nread the documentation for `zero_grad()` by the PyTorch team.\\n\\n```\\nclass Model(LightningModule):\\n    def optimizer_zero_grad(self, epoch, batch_idx, optimizer, optimizer_idx):\\n        optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Things to avoid¶\\n\\n### .item(), .numpy(), .cpu()¶\\n\\nDon’t call `.item()` anywhere in your code. Use `.detach()` instead to remove the connected graph calls. Lightning\\ntakes a great deal of care to be optimized for this.\\n\\n---\\n\\n### empty_cache()¶\\n\\nDon’t call this unnecessarily! Every time you call this ALL your GPUs have to wait to sync.\\n\\n---\\n\\n### Tranfering tensors to device¶\\n\\nLightningModules know what device they are on! Construct tensors on the device directly to avoid CPU->Device transfer.\\n\\n```\\n# bad\\nt = torch.rand(2, 2).cuda()\\n\\n# good (self is LightningModule)\\nt = torch.rand(2, 2, device=self.device)\\n```\\n\\nFor tensors that need to be model attributes, it is best practice to register them as buffers in the modules’s\\n`__init__` method:\\n\\n```\\n# bad\\nself.t = torch.rand(2, 2, device=self.device)\\n\\n# good\\nself.register_buffer(\\\"t\\\", torch.rand(2, 2))\\n```\",\"metadata\":{\"readthedocs-project-slug\":\"pytorch-lightning\",\"google-site-verification\":\"okUst94cAlWSsUsGZTB4xSS4UKTtRV8Nu5XZ9pdd3Aw\",\"readthedocs-resolver-filename\":\"/guides/speed.html\",\"viewport\":\"width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0\",\"readthedocs-http-status\":\"200\",\"generator\":\"Docutils 0.17.1: http://docutils.sourceforge.net/\",\"lang\":\"en\",\"readthedocs-version-slug\":\"1.5.10\"},\"external\":{\"next\":{\"data.html\":{\"title\":\"Managing Data\"}},\"stylesheet\":{\"../_static/main.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"https://use.fontawesome.com/releases/v5.15.2/css/all.css\":{},\"../_static/css/theme.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/sphinx_paramlinks.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/copybutton.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/togglebutton.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"},\"../_static/pygments.css\":{\"type\":\"text/css\"}},\"search\":{\"../search.html\":{\"title\":\"Search\"}},\"shortcut\":{\"../_static/icon.svg\":{}},\"prev\":{\"../starter/lightning_lite.html\":{\"title\":\"LightningLite - Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about PyTorch Lightning and torch.compile integration performance:

## Performance Gains

Compiling your PyTorch model can result in significant speedups, especially on the latest generations of GPUs. According to benchmarks, you can train your model 51% faster on average with AMP on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, and specific examples show even stronger results: ResNet-151 had a performance gain of 74% and ResNet-50 gained 69.2%.

In practical PyTorch Lightning benchmarks on an NVIDIA A100 SXM4 40GB with PyTorch 2.2.0, the speedup was 1.2x with an eager median time of 0.0863 seconds compared to a compiled median time of 0.0709 seconds.

## Integration with PyTorch Lightning

Compiling a LightningModule is as simple as adding one line of code, calling torch.compile(). When you pass the LightningModule to the Trainer, it will automatically also compile the `*_step()` methods.

## Important Caveats

However, users should be aware of limitations. In practice, torch.compile may not work well at first or may be counter-productive to performance. It is not uncommon that torch.compile will produce a significantly slower model or one with higher memory usage. Additionally, the compilation phase itself will take some time, taking up to several minutes.

## Optimization Options

By enabling CUDA Graphs, CUDA will record all computations in a graph and replay it every time forward and backward is called. Enabling CUDA Graphs often results in a significant speedup, but sometimes also increases the memory usage of your model.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about PyTorch Lightning Trainer performance flags related to gradient accumulation and dataloaders:

## Key Performance Flags

### Gradient Accumulation

You can control gradient accumulation using the `accumulate_grad_batches` parameter, with a default of 1 (no accumulation) or accumulate gradients for a specified number of batches like 7. Accumulated gradients runs K small batches of size N before doing a backwards pass, with the effect being a large effective batch size of size KxN.

You can make the `accumulate_grad_batches` value change over time by using the GradientAccumulationScheduler, where the key represents the epoch at which the value for gradient accumulation should be updated.

### Performance Optimization for Gradient Accumulation

When performing gradient accumulation, there is no need to perform grad synchronization during the accumulation phase, and setting sync_grad to False will block this synchronization and improve your training speed.

### DataLoader Configuration

For dataloader performance, you can use multiple dataloader workers, enable torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark, and minimize logging overhead. Additionally, enabling cudnn.benchmark is likely to increase the speed of your system if your input sizes don't change, as the speedup comes from allowing the cudnn auto-tuner to find the best algorithm for the hardware.

### Related Performance Flags

The Trainer includes various flags for customizing training, including parameters for accelerator, devices, precision, and many others that affect performance.

convert_to_float8_training(model, config=float8_config, module_filter_fn=module_filter_fn)\\n\\n        for module in model.modules():\\n            if isinstance(module, (nn.TransformerEncoderLayer, nn.TransformerDecoderLayer)):\\n                fully_shard(module, mesh=self.device_mesh)\\n\\n        fully_shard(model, mesh=self.device_mesh)\\n\\n        self.model = torch.compile(model)\\n```\\n\\nFor a full example, see our FP8 Distributed Transformer example.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Avoid graph breaks¶\\n\\nWhen `torch.compile` looks at the code in your model’s `forward()` or `*_step()` method, it will try to compile as much of the code as possible.\\nIf there are regions in the code that it doesn’t understand, it will introduce a so-called “graph break” that essentially splits the code in optimized and unoptimized parts.\\nGraph breaks aren’t a deal breaker, since the optimized parts should still run faster.\\nBut if you want to get the most out of `torch.compile`, you might want to invest rewriting the problematic section of the code that produces the breaks.\\n\\nYou can check whether your model produces graph breaks by calling `torch.compile` with `fullgraph=True`:\\n\\n```\\n# Force an error if there is a graph break in the model\\nmodel = torch.compile(model, fullgraph=True)\\n```\\n\\nBe aware that the error messages produced here are often quite cryptic, so you will likely have to do some troubleshooting to fully optimize your model.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Avoid recompilation¶\\n\\nAs mentioned before, the compilation of the model happens the first time you call `forward()` or the first time the Trainer calls the `*_step()` methods.\\nAt this point, PyTorch will inspect the input tensor(s) and optimize the compiled code for the particular shape, data type and other properties the input has.\\nIf the shape of the input remains the same across all calls, PyTorch will reuse the compiled code it generated and you will get the best speedup.\\nHowever, if these properties change across subsequent calls to `forward()`/`*_step()`, PyTorch will be forced to recompile the model for the new shapes, and this will significantly slow down your training if it happens on every iteration.\\n\\n__When your training suddenly becomes slow, it’s probably because PyTorch is recompiling the model!__\\nHere are some common scenarios when this can happen:\\n\\n- You are using dataset with different inputs or shapes for validation than for training, causing a recompilation whenever the Trainer switches between training and validation.\\n- Your dataset size is not divisible by the batch size, and the dataloader has `drop_last=False` (the default).\\n  The last batch in your training loop will be smaller and trigger a recompilation.\\n\\nIdeally, you should try to make the input shape(s) to `forward()` static.\\nHowever, when this is not possible, you can request PyTorch to compile the code by taking into account possible changes to the input shapes.\\n\\n```\\n# On PyTorch < 2.2\\nmodel = torch.compile(model, dynamic=True)\\n```\\n\\nA model compiled with `dynamic=True` will typically be slower than a model compiled with static shapes, but it will avoid the extreme cost of recompilation every iteration.\\nOn PyTorch 2.2 and later, `torch.compile` will detect dynamism automatically and you should no longer need to set this.\\n\\nIf you still see recompilation issues after dealing with the aforementioned cases, there is a Compile Profiler in PyTorch for further investigation.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Experiment with compilation options¶\\n\\nThere are optional settings that, depending on your model, can give additional speedups.\\n\\n__CUDA Graphs:__ By enabling CUDA Graphs, CUDA will record all computations in a graph and replay it every time forward and backward is called.\\nThe requirement is that your model must be static, i.e., the input shape must not change and your model must execute the same operations every time.\\nEnabling CUDA Graphs often results in a significant speedup, but sometimes also increases the memory usage of your model.\\n\\n```\\n# Enable CUDA Graphs\\ncompiled_model = torch.compile(model, mode=\\\"reduce-overhead\\\")\\n\\n# This does the same\\ncompiled_model = torch.compile(model, options={\\\"triton.cudagraphs\\\": True})\\n```\\n\\n__Shape padding:__ The specific shape/size of the tensors involved in the computation of your model (input, activations, weights, gradients, etc.) can have an impact on the performance.\\nWith shape padding enabled, `torch.compile` can extend the tensors by padding to a size that gives a better memory alignment.\\nNaturally, the tradeoff here is that it will consume a bit more memory.\\n\\n```\\n# Default is False\\ncompiled_model = torch.compile(model, options={\\\"shape_padding\\\": True})\\n```\\n\\nYou can find a full list of compile options in the PyTorch documentation.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## A note about torch.compile in practice¶\\n\\nIn practice, you will find that `torch.compile` may not work well at first or may be counter-productive to performance.\\nCompilation may fail with cryptic error messages that are hard to debug, luckily the PyTorch team is responsive and it’s likely that messaging will improve in time.\\nIt is not uncommon that `torch.compile` will produce a significantly _slower_ model or one with higher memory usage. You’ll need to invest time in this phase if the model is not among the ones that have a happy path.\\nAs a note, the compilation phase itself will take some time, taking up to several minutes.\\nFor these reasons, we recommend that you don’t invest too much time trying to apply `torch.compile` during development, and rather evaluate its effectiveness toward the end when you are about to launch long-running, expensive experiments.\\nAlways compare the speed and memory usage of the compiled model against the original model!\\n\\nFor a thorough troubleshooting guide, see Torch.compile: the missing manual.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Limitations¶\\n\\nThere are a few limitations you should be aware of when using `torch.compile` __in conjunction with the Trainer__:\\n\\n- The Trainer currently does not reapply `torch.compile` over `DDPStrategy` and `FSDPStrategy`, meaning distributed operations can’t benefit from speed ups at the moment.\\n  This limitation can be avoided by using `ModelParallelStrategy`, as described in Apply torch.compile in configure_model above.\\n- In some cases, using `self.log()` in your LightningModule will cause compilation errors.\\n  Until addressed, you can work around these issues by applying `torch.compile` to the submodule(s) of your LightningModule rather than to the entire LightningModule at once.\\n\\n  ```\\n  import lightning as L\\n\\n  class MyLightningModule(L.LightningModule):\\n      def __init__(self):\\n          super().__init__()\\n          self.model = MySubModule()\\n          self.model = torch.compile(self.model)\\n          ...\\n  ```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Additional Resources¶\\n\\nHere are a few resources for further reading after you complete this tutorial:\\n\\n- PyTorch 2.0 Paper\\n- GenAI with PyTorch 2.0 blog post series\\n- Training Production AI Models with PyTorch 2.0\\n- Empowering Models with Performance: The Art of Generalized Model Transformation Approach\\n- Torch.compile: the missing "{\"title\":\"Effective Training Techniques — PyTorch Lightning 2.6.1 documentation\",\"url\":\"https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable/advanced/training_tricks.html\",\"content\":\"Lightning implements various techniques to help during training that can help make the training smoother.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Accumulate Gradients¶\\n\\nAccumulated gradients run K small batches of size `N` before doing a backward pass. The effect is a large effective batch size of size `KxN`, where `N` is the batch size.\\nInternally it doesn’t stack up the batches and do a forward pass rather it accumulates the gradients for K batches and then do an `optimizer.step` to make sure the\\neffective batch size is increased but there is no memory overhead.\\n\\nWarning\\n\\nWhen using distributed training for eg. DDP, with let’s say with `P` devices, each device accumulates independently i.e. it stores the gradients\\nafter each `loss.backward()` and doesn’t sync the gradients across the devices until we call `optimizer.step()`. So for each accumulation\\nstep, the effective batch size on each device will remain `N*K` but right before the `optimizer.step()`, the gradient sync will make the effective\\nbatch size as `P*N*K`. For DP, since the batch is split across devices, the final effective batch size will be `N*K`.\\n\\n```\\n# DEFAULT (ie: no accumulated grads)\\ntrainer = Trainer(accumulate_grad_batches=1)\\n\\n# Accumulate gradients for 7 batches\\ntrainer = Trainer(accumulate_grad_batches=7)\\n```\\n\\nOptionally, you can make the `accumulate_grad_batches` value change over time by using the `GradientAccumulationScheduler`.\\nPass in a scheduling dictionary, where the key represents the epoch at which the value for gradient accumulation should be updated.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.callbacks import GradientAccumulationScheduler\\n\\n# till 5th epoch, it will accumulate every 8 batches. From 5th epoch\\n# till 9th epoch it will accumulate every 4 batches and after that no accumulation\\n# will happen. Note that you need to use zero-indexed epoch keys here\\naccumulator = GradientAccumulationScheduler(scheduling={0: 8, 4: 4, 8: 1})\\ntrainer = Trainer(callbacks=accumulator)\\n```\\n\\nNote: Not all strategies and accelerators support variable gradient accumulation windows.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Gradient Clipping¶\\n\\nGradient clipping can be enabled to avoid exploding gradients. By default, this will clip the gradient norm by calling\\n`torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_()` computed over all model parameters together.\\nIf the Trainer’s `gradient_clip_algorithm` is set to `'value'` (`'norm'` by default), this will use instead\\n`torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_value_()` for each parameter instead.\\n\\nNote\\n\\nIf using mixed precision, the `gradient_clip_val` does not need to be changed as the gradients are unscaled\\nbefore applying the clipping function.\\n\\n```\\n# DEFAULT (ie: don't clip)\\ntrainer = Trainer(gradient_clip_val=0)\\n\\n# clip gradients' global norm to <=0.5 using gradient_clip_algorithm='norm' by default\\ntrainer = Trainer(gradient_clip_val=0.5)\\n\\n# clip gradients' maximum magnitude to <=0.5\\ntrainer = Trainer(gradient_clip_val=0.5, gradient_clip_algorithm=\\\"value\\\")\\n```\\n\\nRead more about Configuring Gradient Clipping for advanced use cases.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Weight Averaging¶\\n\\nWeight averaging methods such as Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA) and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) can make your\\nmodels generalize better at virtually no additional cost. Averaging smooths the loss landscape thus making it harder to\\nend up in a local minimum during optimization.\\n\\nLightning provides two callbacks to facilitate weight averaging. `WeightAveraging`\\nis a generic callback that wraps the\\nAveragedModel class from\\nPyTorch. It allows SWA, EMA, or a custom averaging strategy to be used. By default, it updates the weights after every\\nstep, but it can be customized to update at specific steps or epochs by overriding the should_update() method.\\n\\nThe older `StochasticWeightAveraging` callback is specific to SWA. It starts the SWA\\nprocedure after a certain number of epochs and always runs on every epoch. Additionally, it switches to a constant\\nlearning rate schedule (SWALR) when the\\nprocedure starts.\\n\\nSee also\\n\\nFor a more detailed explanation of SWA and how it works, read\\nthis post by the PyTorch team.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.callbacks import StochasticWeightAveraging, WeightAveraging\\nfrom torch.optim.swa_utils import get_ema_avg_fn\\n\\n# Enable Exponential Moving Average after 100 steps\\nclass EMAWeightAveraging(WeightAveraging):\\n    def __init__(self):\\n        super().__init__(avg_fn=get_ema_avg_fn())\\n    def should_update(self, step_idx=None, epoch_idx=None):\\n        return (step_idx is not None) and (step_idx >= 100)\\ntrainer = Trainer(callbacks=EMAWeightAveraging())\\n\\n# Enable Stochastic Weight Averaging after 10 epochs with learning rate 0.01\\ntrainer = Trainer(callbacks=StochasticWeightAveraging(swa_epoch_start=10, swa_lrs=0.01))\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Batch Size Finder¶\\n\\nAuto-scaling of batch size can be enabled to find the largest batch size that fits into\\nmemory. Large batch size often yields a better estimation of the gradients, but may also result in\\nlonger training time. Inspired by https://github.com/BlackHC/toma.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.tuner import Tuner\\n\\n# Create a tuner for the trainer\\ntrainer = Trainer(...)\\ntuner = Tuner(trainer)\\n\\n# Auto-scale batch size by growing it exponentially (default)\\ntuner.scale_batch_size(model, mode=\\\"power\\\")\\n\\n# Auto-scale batch size with binary search\\ntuner.scale_batch_size(model, mode=\\\"binsearch\\\")\\n\\n# Fit as normal with new batch size\\ntrainer.fit(model)\\n```\\n\\nCurrently, this feature supports two modes `'power'` scaling and `'binsearch'`\\nscaling. In `'power'` scaling, starting from a batch size of 1 keeps doubling\\nthe batch size until an out-of-memory (OOM) error is encountered. Setting the\\nargument to `'binsearch'` will initially also try doubling the batch size until\\nit encounters an OOM, after which it will do a binary search that will finetune the\\nbatch size. Additionally, it should be noted that the batch size scaler cannot\\nsearch for batch sizes larger than the size of the training dataset.\\n\\nNote\\n\\nThis feature expects that a `batch_size` field is either located as a model attribute\\ni.e. `model.batch_size` or as a field in your `hparams` i.e. `model.hparams.batch_size`.\\nSimilarly it can work with datamodules too. The field should exist and will be updated by\\nthe results of this algorithm. Additionally, your `train_dataloader()` method should depend\\non this field for this feature to work i.e.\\n\\n```\\n# using LightningModule\\nclass LitModel(LightningModule):\\n    def __init__(self, batch_size):\\n        super().__init__()\\n        self.save_hyperparameters()\\n        # or\\n        self.batch_size = batch_size\\n\\n    def train_dataloader(self):\\n        return DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=self.batch_size | self.hparams.batch_size)\\n\\nmodel = LitModel(batch_size=32)\\ntrainer = Trainer(...)\\ntuner = Tuner(trainer)\\ntuner.scale_batch_size(model)\\n\\n# using LightningDataModule\\nclass LitDataModule(LightningDataModule):\\n    def __init__(self, batch_size):\\n        super().__init__()\\n        self.save_hyperparameters()\\n        # or\\n        self.batch_size = batch_size\\n\\n    def train_dataloader(self):\\n        return DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=self.batch_size | self.hparams.batch_size)\\n\\nmodel = MyModel()\\ndatamodule = LitDataModule(batch_size=32)\\n\\ntrainer = Trainer(...)\\ntuner = Tuner(trainer)\\ntuner.scale_batch_size(model, datamodule=datamodule)\\n```\\n\\nNote that the `train_dataloader` can be either part of\\nthe `LightningModule` or `LightningDataModule`\\nas shown above. If both the `LightningModule`\\nand the `LightningDataModule` contain a `train_dataloader`,\\nthe `LightningDataModule` takes precedence.\\n\\nThe algorithm in short works by:\\n:   1. Dumping the current state of the model and trainer\\n    2. Iteratively until convergence or maximum number of tries `max_trials` (default 25) has been reached:\\n       :   - Call `fit()` method of trainer. This evaluates `steps_per_trial` (default 3) number of\\n             optimization steps. Each training step can trigger an OOM error if the tensors\\n             (training batch, weights, gradients, etc.) allocated during the steps have a\\n             too large memory footprint.\\n           - If an OOM error is encountered, decrease batch size else increase it.\\n             How much the batch size is increased/decreased is determined by the chosen\\n             strategy.\\n    3. The found batch size is saved to either `model.batch_size` or `model.hparams.batch_size`\\n    4. Restore the initial state of model and trainer\\n\\nWarning\\n\\nBatch size finder is not yet supported for DDP or any of its variations, it is coming soon.\\n\\n### Customizing Batch Size Finder¶\\n\\n1. You can also customize the `BatchSizeFinder` callback to run\\n   at different epochs. This feature is useful while fine-tuning models since you can’t always use the same batch size after\\n   unfreezing the backbone.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.callbacks import BatchSizeFinder\\n\\nclass FineTuneBatchSizeFinder(BatchSizeFinder):\\n    def __init__(self, milestones, *args, **kwargs):\\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\\n        self.milestones = milestones\\n\\n    def on_fit_start(self, *args, **kwargs):\\n        return\\n\\n    def on_train_epoch_start(self, trainer, pl_module):\\n        if trainer.current_epoch in self.milestones or trainer.current_epoch == 0:\\n            self.scale_batch_size(trainer, pl_module)\\n\\ntrainer = Trainer(callbacks=[FineTuneBatchSizeFinder(milestones=(5, 10))])\\ntrainer.fit(...)\\n```\\n\\n2. Run batch size finder for `validate`/`test`/`predict`.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.callbacks import BatchSizeFinder\\n\\nclass EvalBatchSizeFinder(BatchSizeFinder):\\n    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):\\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\\n\\n    def on_fit_start(self, *args, **kwargs):\\n        return\\n\\n    def on_test_start(self, trainer, pl_module):\\n        self.scale_batch_size(trainer, pl_module)\\n\\ntrainer = Trainer(callbacks=[EvalBatchSizeFinder()])\\ntrainer.test(...)\\n```\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Learning Rate Finder¶\\n\\nFor training deep neural networks, selecting a good learning rate is essential\\nfor both better performance and faster convergence. Even optimizers such as\\n`Adam` that are self-adjusting the learning rate can benefit from more optimal\\nchoices.\\n\\nTo reduce the amount of guesswork concerning choosing a good initial learning\\nrate, a learning rate finder can be used. As described in this paper\\na learning rate finder does a small run where the learning rate is increased\\nafter each processed batch and the corresponding loss is logged. The result of\\nthis is a `lr` vs. `loss` plot that can be used as guidance for choosing an optimal\\ninitial learning rate.\\n\\nWarning\\n\\nFor the moment, this feature only works with models having a single optimizer.\\n\\nNote\\n\\nWith DDP: Since all the processes run in isolation, only process with `global_rank=0` will make the decision to stop the\\nlearning rate finder and broadcast its results to all other ranks. That means, at the end of LR finder, each process will be running with\\nthe learning rate found on `global_rank=0`.\\n\\n### Using Lightning’s built-in LR finder¶\\n\\nTo enable the learning rate finder, your lightning module needs to\\nhave a `learning_rate` or `lr` attribute (or as a field in your `hparams` i.e.\\n`hparams.learning_rate` or `hparams.lr`). Then, create the `Tuner` via `tuner = Tuner(trainer)`\\nand call `tuner.lr_find(model)` to run the LR finder.\\nThe suggested `learning_rate` will be written to the console and will be automatically\\nset to your lightning module, which can be accessed\\nvia `self.learning_rate` or `self.lr`.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.tuner import Tuner\\n\\nclass LitModel(LightningModule):\\n    def __init__(self, learning_rate):\\n        super().__init__()\\n        self.learning_rate = learning_rate\\n        self.model = Model(...)\\n\\n    def configure_optimizers(self):\\n        return Adam(self.parameters(), lr=(self.lr or self.learning_rate))\\n\\nmodel = LitModel()\\ntrainer = Trainer(...)\\n\\n# Create a Tuner\\ntuner = Tuner(trainer)\\n\\n# finds learning rate automatically\\n# sets hparams.lr or hparams.learning_rate to that learning rate\\ntuner.lr_find(model)\\n```\\n\\nIf your model is using an arbitrary value instead of `self.lr` or `self.learning_rate`, set that value in `lr_find`:\\n\\n```\\nmodel = LitModel()\\ntrainer = Trainer(...)\\ntuner = Tuner(trainer)\\n\\n# to set to your own hparams.my_value\\ntuner.lr_find(model, attr_name=\\\"my_value\\\")\\n```\\n\\nYou can also inspect the results of the learning rate finder or just play around\\nwith the parameters of the algorithm. A typical example of this would look like:\\n\\n```\\nmodel = MyModelClass(hparams)\\ntrainer = Trainer()\\ntuner = Tuner(trainer)\\n\\n# Run learning rate finder\\nlr_finder = tuner.lr_find(model)\\n\\n# Results can be found in\\nprint(lr_finder.results)\\n\\n# Plot with\\nfig = lr_finder.plot(suggest=True)\\nfig.show()\\n\\n# Pick point based on plot, or get suggestion\\nnew_lr = lr_finder.suggestion()\\n\\n# update hparams of the model\\nmodel.hparams.lr = new_lr\\n\\n# Fit model\\ntrainer.fit(model)\\n```\\n\\nThe figure produced by `lr_finder.plot()` should look something like the figure\\nbelow. It is recommended to not pick the learning rate that achieves the lowest\\nloss, but instead something in the middle of the sharpest downward slope (red point).\\nThis is the point returned py `lr_finder.suggestion()`.\\n\\n### Customizing Learning Rate Finder¶\\n\\nYou can also customize the `LearningRateFinder` callback to run at different epochs. This feature is useful while fine-tuning models.\\n\\n```\\nfrom lightning.pytorch.callbacks import LearningRateFinder\\n\\nclass FineTuneLearningRateFinder(LearningRateFinder):\\n    def __init__(self, milestones, *args, **kwargs):\\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\\n        self.milestones = milestones\\n\\n    def on_fit_start(self, *args, **kwargs):\\n        return\\n\\n    def on_train_epoch_start(self, trainer, pl_module):\\n        if trainer.current_epoch in self.milestones or trainer.current_epoch == 0:\\n            self.lr_find(trainer, pl_module)\\n\\ntrainer = Trainer(callbacks=[FineTuneLearningRateFinder(milestones=(5, 10))])\\ntrainer.fit(...)\\n```\\n\\n![Image 1: ../_images/lr_finder.png](https://lightning.ai/_images/lr_finder.png)\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Advanced GPU Optimizations¶\\n\\nWhen training on single or multiple GPU machines, Lightning offers a host of advanced optimizations to improve throughput, memory efficiency, and model scaling.\\nRefer to Advanced GPU Optimized Training for more details.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Sharing Datasets Across Process Boundaries¶\\n\\nThe `LightningDataModule` class provides an organized way to decouple data loading from training logic, with `prepare_data()` being used for downloading and pre-processing the dataset on a single process, and `setup()` loading the pre-processed data for each process individually:\\n\\n```\\nclass MNISTDataModule(L.LightningDataModule):\\n    def prepare_data(self):\\n        MNIST(self.data_dir, download=True)\\n\\n    def setup(self, stage: str):\\n        self.mnist = MNIST(self.data_dir)\\n\\n    def train_loader(self):\\n        return DataLoader(self.mnist, batch_size=128)\\n```\\n\\nHowever, for in-memory datasets, that means that each process will hold a (redundant) replica of the dataset in memory, which may be impractical when using many processes while utilizing datasets that nearly fit into CPU memory, as the memory consumption will scale up linearly with the number of processes.\\nFor example, when training Graph Neural Networks, a common strategy is to load the entire graph into CPU memory for fast access to the entire graph structure and its features, and to then perform neighbor sampling to obtain mini-batches that fit onto the GPU.\\n\\nA simple way to prevent redundant dataset replicas is to rely on `torch.multiprocessing` to share the data automatically between spawned processes via shared memory.\\nFor this, all data pre-loading should be done on the main process inside `DataModule.__init__()`. As a result, all tensor-data will get automatically shared when using the `'ddp_spawn'` strategy.\\n\\nWarning\\n\\n`torch.multiprocessing` will send a handle of each individual tensor to other processes.\\nIn order to prevent any errors due to too many open file handles, try to reduce the number of tensors to share, _e.g._, by stacking your data into a single tensor.\\n\\n```\\nclass I've performed a web search on PyTorch Lightning Trainer accelerator strategy precision performance configuration. Here are the key findings:

## Trainer Configuration Parameters

The PyTorch Lightning Trainer includes parameters for accelerator, strategy, devices, precision, and many other configuration options.

## Accelerator

The accelerator parameter supports different accelerator types ("cpu", "gpu", "tpu", "hpu", "mps", "auto") as well as custom accelerator instances. Setting `accelerator='auto'` allows the framework to choose between CPU, GPU, or TPU based on availability.

## Strategy

Passing training strategies (e.g., "ddp") to accelerator has been deprecated and the strategy argument should be used instead. Examples include training with DistributedDataParallel (DDP) strategy on multiple GPUs, DDP Spawn strategy, and DeepSpeed strategy.

## Precision Configuration

Precision supports double precision (64, '64' or '64-true'), full precision (32, '32' or '32-true'), 16bit mixed precision (16, '16', '16-mixed') or bfloat16 mixed precision ('bf16', 'bf16-mixed').

## Performance Benefits

Mixed precision combines the use of both 32 and 16-bit floating points to reduce memory footprint during model training, resulting in improved performance, achieving up to +3X speedups on modern GPUs. Mixed precision training delivers significant computational speedup by conducting operations in half-precision format while keeping minimum information in single-precision to maintain crucial information in important areas of the network.

Now I have comprehensive documentation on PyTorch Lightning performance optimizations. Let me compile a concise summary of the key options:

## PyTorch Lightning Performance Optimization Summary

Based on the Lightning AI documentation, here are the key performance optimization options available in PyTorch Lightning:

### **1. Mixed Precision Training**
Combines FP32 and lower-bit floating points (FP16/BF16) to reduce memory footprint and increase performance, delivering up to 3x speedups on modern GPUs.

**Trainer flags:**
- `precision="16-mixed"` - FP16 mixed precision with gradient scaling
- `precision="bf16-mixed"` - BF16 mixed precision (better numerical stability, requires Ampere+ GPUs)
- `precision="16-true"` - True FP16 (weights cast to float16)
- `precision="bf16-true"` - True BF16 (weights cast to bfloat16)
- `precision="transformer-engine"` - FP8 mixed precision via NVIDIA TransformerEngine (Hopper GPUs only)

**LightningModule hook:**
```python
with trainer.init_module():
    model = MyModel()  # Initialize directly on device in target precision
```

### **2. torch.compile Integration**
JIT-compiles the model and training/validation/test step methods for significant speedups (typically 1.2x on A100).

**Configuration:**
```python
model = torch.compile(model)
trainer = Trainer()
trainer.fit(model)
```

**Compile options:**
- `mode="reduce-overhead"` or `options={"triton.cudagraphs": True}` - Enable CUDA Graphs for static models
- `options={"shape_padding": True}` - Pad tensor shapes for better memory alignment
- `dynamic=True` - Handle variable input shapes (slower but avoids recompilation on PyTorch < 2.2)
- `fullgraph=True` - Detect graph breaks during debugging

**Caveats:** Compilation takes time (minutes), may be slower/use more memory, and doesn't work well with DDP/FSDP in current versions.

### **3. Gradient Accumulation**
Accumulate gradients over K batches before optimizer step to simulate larger batch sizes without memory overhead.

**Trainer flags:**
```python
trainer = Trainer(accumulate_grad_batches=7)
```

**Dynamic accumulation schedule:**
```python
from lightning.pytorch.callbacks import GradientAccumulationScheduler
accumulator = GradientAccumulationScheduler(scheduling={0: 8, 4: 4, 8: 1})
trainer = Trainer(callbacks=accumulator)
```

**LightningModule optimization for DDP:**
```python
# In training_step with manual optimization
with optimizer.toggle_model(sync_grad=is_last_batch_to_accumulate):
    # Prevents grad synchronization during accumulation phase
    ...
```

### **4. DataLoader Optimization**
Use multi-worker data loading with GPU memory pinning.

**DataLoader configuration:**
```python
DataLoader(dataset, num_workers=8, pin_memory=True)
```

**Guidance:**
- Start `num_workers` = number of CPU cores, increase gradually until throughput plateaus
- `num_workers=0` bottlenecks training (avoids multiprocessing overhead but slow)
- Watch RAM consumption when increasing workers
- For DDP, use `strategy="ddp"` (not `ddp_spawn`) to support `num_workers > 0`

### **5. Trainer Performance Flags**

**Distributed training:**
- `accelerator="gpu"` - Use GPU acceleration
- `strategy="ddp"` - DistributedDataParallel (preferred over "dp")
- `strategy=DDPPlugin(find_unused_parameters=False)` - Skip unused parameter checks for DDP

**Gradient management:**
```python
class Model(LightningModule):
    def optimizer_zero_grad(self, epoch, batch_idx, optimizer, optimizer_idx):
        optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)  # Modestly faster than zero_grad()
```

**Validation frequency (reduce during large training runs):**
```python
trainer = Trainer(check_val_every_n_epoch=5)  # Default is 1
trainer = Trainer(val_check_interval=0.25)    # Check every 0.25 of epoch
```

**Batch size tuning:**
```python
from lightning.pytorch.tuner import Tuner
tuner = Tuner(trainer)
tuner.scale_batch_size(model, mode="power")  # Auto-find max batch size
```

### **6. Advanced Precision Techniques**

**Float8 Quantization (TransformerEngine on Hopper):**
```python
from lightning.pytorch.plugins import TransformerEnginePrecision
recipe = {"fp8_format": "HYBRID", "amax_history_len": 16, "amax_compute_algo": "max"}
precision = TransformerEnginePrecision(weights_dtype=torch.bfloat16, recipe=recipe)
trainer = Trainer(plugins=precision)
```

**Weight Quantization (bitsandbytes):**
```python
from lightning.pytorch.plugins import BitsandbytesPrecision
precision = BitsandbytesPrecision(mode="nf4-dq", dtype=torch.float16)
trainer = Trainer(plugins=precision)
```

### **7. Things to Avoid**
- `.item()`, `.numpy()`, `.cpu()` on GPU tensors (use `.detach()` instead)
- Unnecessary `empty_cache()` calls (forces all GPUs to sync)
- Manual device transfers with `.cuda()` (use `device=self.device` instead)
- Store tensors as attributes (use `register_buffer()` instead)

### **8. Multi-node Optimization (DDP)**
Set NCCL environment variables for better cluster performance:
```bash
export NCCL_NSOCKS_PERTHREAD=4
export NCCL_SOCKET_NTHREADS=2
```

- [N-Bit Precision Documentation](https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable/common/precision_intermediate.html)
- [Speed Up Model Training](https://pytorch-lightning.readthedocs.io/en/1.5.10/guides/speed.html)
- [torch.compile Integration](https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable/advanced/compile.html)
- [Effective Training Techniques](https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable/advanced/training_tricks.html)
- [Trainer Documentation](https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable/common/trainer.html)
- [Mixed Precision Training](https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/1.5.9/advanced/mixed_precision.html)
